# Alex Nguyen - Managing Director & Co-founder

## My Role at NexusPoint Systems

I've been living and breathing NexusPoint for the past eight years since my business partner David Chen and I founded it back in 2016. As Managing Director, I'm essentially wearing three hats these days – chief strategist, client relationship guardian, and increasingly, the person making the tough decisions about where we invest our limited resources.

My typical week is split between client meetings, strategic planning with our leadership team, and what I call "firefighting" – those unexpected issues that need the founder's touch. I spend about 40% of my time with existing clients, particularly our tier-one accounts like Meridian Mining Services, Perth Commercial Properties, and the University of Western Australia's satellite campus operations. These relationships took years to build, and I'm not about to let them slide while we chase new opportunities.

The other 60% is split between internal strategy work and business development. I'm heavily involved in our security practice development – not hands-on technical work anymore, that ship sailed about three years ago – but ensuring Marcus Webb has what he needs to build something truly competitive. I also spend considerable time on vendor relationships, particularly with Microsoft and AWS. Those partnerships aren't just badges on our website; they're critical to our cost structure and technical capabilities.

What's changed dramatically in the past 18 months is how much time I'm spending on talent acquisition and retention. In the old days, I could focus on growing client relationships and let the technical work sort itself out. Now, with everyone from miners to accounting firms demanding advanced security capabilities, I'm constantly balancing what we can realistically deliver against what clients need.

## Facts I Know

The numbers tell the story of where we've been and where we're heading. We're tracking toward $6.2 million in revenue this financial year, which represents 22% growth over last year. That might sound impressive, but it's actually slower growth than we've seen in previous years – a direct result of being more selective about contracts we can actually deliver on.

Our managed services base is rock solid: 127 active contracts as of last month, with 65% of our revenue coming from recurring monthly agreements. Our uptime SLA of 99.7% isn't just marketing speak – Priya Sharma runs our NOC like a military operation, and our average first response time is actually 11.3 minutes, not the 12 minutes we advertise. I know this because I review our service delivery metrics every Monday morning at 7 AM, without fail.

The security practice is where things get interesting. Marcus joined us 14 months ago from Fortinet, and since then our security revenue has grown 40% year-over-year. We're at $1.8 million in security-specific revenue this year, but that includes a lot of basic services like endpoint protection and security awareness training. The sophisticated stuff – security operations, incident response, compliance frameworks – that's where the real money is, and honestly, where we're still building our capabilities.

Our biggest financial reality is cash flow seasonality. December and January are always tight because many clients defer infrastructure investments over the holiday period, but our fixed costs – particularly salaries for our 35 employees – don't change. Tanya Brooks, our Finance Manager, has built us a cash flow buffer of about $450,000, which sounds comfortable until you realize we're looking at potentially hiring four or five security specialists at $120,000+ each.

The Microsoft Gold Partnership brings us about 15% margin improvement on licensing, and our AWS Select Partnership gets us better support response times and some co-marketing funds. But the real value is technical credibility. When I'm sitting across from a CFO explaining why they need to invest $180,000 in a cloud migration, those partnership logos matter.

## My Opinions

Here's what I believe after eight years of building this business: security is becoming the core of what we do, not an add-on. I've seen the shift in client conversations over the past two years. Used to be, they'd call us when their server died or they needed to add new users. Now, they're calling because they're terrified of ransomware, or because their insurance company is demanding security assessments, or because their largest client won't sign a contract without cybersecurity certifications.

That said, I'm absolutely convinced that our reputation for reliability and straight talk is what differentiates us from the larger players like Datacom or smaller shops that promise everything and deliver chaos. We don't oversell. When a client asks if we can implement a security operations center next month, I don't say yes just to win the deal. I explain what's realistic, what it will cost, and how long it will actually take. This approach has cost us some deals, but it's also why our client retention rate is 94%.

The mining contract everyone's talking about – it's a $2.3 million, three-year security overhaul for Meridian's entire operation – could absolutely transform our business. But I'm genuinely worried about what it would do to our team. We're already asking people to work harder than I'm comfortable with. Liam Foster is brilliant, but he's already juggling architecture work for six major projects. Our service desk team is handling 23% more tickets than this time last year with the same headcount.

I have a fundamental disagreement with the "grow at all costs" mentality I see from some of our competitors. I'd rather grow at 20% annually and maintain our reputation than double our revenue and burn out our people or disappoint clients. Maybe that makes me conservative, but I've seen what happens to companies that sacrifice quality for growth – they don't survive long-term.

The Perth talent market is absolutely brutal right now. Every mining company, bank, and government agency is competing for the same security professionals we need. I've had three offers rejected in the past two months because candidates got counter-offers 30% higher than what we could justify. It's frustrating, but throwing money at the problem isn't sustainable for a company our size.

## Working at NexusPoint

The culture we've built here is something I'm genuinely proud of, but I know it's under strain right now. When we were 20 people, everyone knew everyone. We could make decisions quickly, communicate directly, and maintain that startup energy. At 35 people, we're hitting the point where we need more structure, but I'm trying to preserve what made us successful in the first place.

Priya Sharma is absolutely essential to our operations. She joined us three years ago from Telstra and brought a level of process discipline that we desperately needed. Sometimes I think she worries more about client SLAs than I do, which is saying something. We don't always agree – she wants to standardize everything, while I believe some clients need customized approaches – but I trust her judgment completely.

Marcus Webb is still finding his place, if I'm being honest. His technical skills are exactly what we need for the security practice, but he comes from a large enterprise background where resources weren't a constraint. Sometimes I need to remind him that we can't implement everything at once. That said, he's been invaluable in structuring our security service offerings and building credibility with clients who need enterprise-grade solutions.

Tanya Brooks keeps me grounded financially. She's not afraid to tell me when an idea doesn't make economic sense, which I appreciate. Recently, she pushed back on my suggestion to hire a dedicated sales person, arguing that our growth is constrained by delivery capacity, not sales pipeline. She was right, of course, but it took me a few weeks to admit it.

Jordan Reeves has been crucial as we've navigated the growing pains of the past year. People issues that I used to handle directly now go through Jordan, and they've helped us implement performance review processes and career development frameworks that we never had time to build properly.

What I value most about our team is the commitment to doing right by clients, even when it's not the easy path. Last month, Sam Okoro's team identified a potential security issue with one of our managed service clients before it became a problem. They could have just patched it quietly, but instead they proactively called the client, explained the risk, and recommended additional security measures. That's the kind of thinking that builds long-term relationships.

## Current Challenges

The immediate challenge is capacity management. We're not saying no to enough opportunities, and I can see the stress building across the technical team. Liam Foster is working on six major projects simultaneously, which is not sustainable. Priya keeps asking for more service delivery resources, and Marcus needs security analysts to build the SOC capability clients are demanding.

The Meridian mining contract is both our biggest opportunity and biggest risk. If we win it, it would represent about 15% of our total revenue and establish us as a serious security player in the WA market. But if we screw it up, or if we sacrifice other client relationships to deliver it, we could damage everything we've built. I've told Marcus we need to be absolutely certain we can deliver before we submit our proposal next month.

Financially, we're in a weird position. We're profitable and growing, but every growth opportunity requires upfront investment in people or technology that strains our cash flow. The security practice needs specialized tools – SIEM platforms, threat intelligence feeds, security orchestration tools – that cost $15,000-30,000 per month each. These are necessary investments, but they hit our bottom line before we see revenue.

The competitive landscape is shifting faster than I anticipated. Three new cybersecurity firms have opened in Perth in the past year, and established players like Dimension Data are aggressively pursuing our target market. We can't compete on price with offshore providers, and we can't match the resources of multinational firms, so we need to be smarter about where we focus.

Internal communication is becoming more challenging as we grow. Information that used to flow naturally now requires structured meetings and documentation. I find myself spending more time in internal meetings and less time with clients, which feels backward but seems inevitable.

The biggest challenge, though, is maintaining our culture and standards while growing fast enough to capitalize on market opportunities. I wake up most mornings thinking about this balance. Push too hard, and we risk burning out our best people or disappointing clients. Move too slowly, and we miss the security market opportunity that could define our next decade.

I'm confident we'll figure it out – we always have – but I'd be lying if I said it wasn't keeping me up at night more than I'd like to admit.
